coredata.dc:title: Oxygen vacancy-abundant carbon quantum dots as superfast hole transport channel for vastly improving surface charge transfer efficiency of BiVO4 photoanode
coredata.dc:creator: Zhou, Tingsheng
Wang, Jiachen
Zhang, Yan
Zhou, Changhui
Bai, Jing
Li, Jinhua
Zhou, Baoxue
coredata.dc:description: The efficiency of hole extraction and transfer at electrode/electrolyte interface is one of the most important bottlenecks of BiVO4 photoanodes for photoelectrochemical (PEC) water splitting. Here, a huge improvement of surface charge transfer efficiency (η surface) of BiVO4 photoanode was achieved by surface modification of oxygen vacancy-abundant carbon quantum dots (OV-CQDs), in which the OV-CQDs serve as superfast hole transport channel for the fact that the abundant OV in OV-CQDs could induce the outward driving force for hole trapping and migration at electrode/electrolyte interface. The OV-CQDs/BiVO4 shows the η surface value of 74.3% at 0.65 V vs. RHE (VRHE), which is 7.1 and 3.3 times higher than BiVO4 and CQDs/BiVO4, respectively. Besides, the OV-CQDs efficiently promote the bulk charge separation and UV-vis light harvesting of BiVO4. Therefore, the OV-CQDs/BiVO4 exhibits remarkable photocurrent densities of 2.76 mA cm−2 at 0.65 VRHE and 4.01 mA cm−2 at 1.23 VRHE, which are 12.5 and 3.4 times higher than BiVO4, 3.5 and 2.6 times higher than CQDs/BiVO4, respectively.
coredata.dcterms:subject: BiVO4 photoanode
OV-CQDs
Hole transport channel
Surface charge transfer efficiency
OER kinetics

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articlenumber articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table e-component body mmlmath acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor highlightsabst primabst ref specialabst

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of OV-CQDs/BiVO4 and CQDs/BiVO4 photoanodes

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image and size distribution of OV-CQDs (inset: HTEM of OV-CQDs), (b) EPR and (c) FTIR spectra of CQDs and OV-CQDs, (d) top-view and (e) cross-section SEM images of OV-CQDs/BiVO4, (f) TEM image of OV-CQDs/BiVO4, (g) HTEM image of the selected doted box in (f) by turning 90° clockwise, (h) elemental mapping of OV-CQDs/BiVO4.
High-resolution (a) Bi 4f, (b) V 2p, (c) O 1 s and (d) C 1 s spectra of BiVO4, CQDs/BiVO4 and OV-CQDs/BiVO4.
UV-vis absorption spectra of BiVO4, CQDs/BiVO4 and OV-CQDs/BiVO4. The inset is the Tauc plot of BiVO4.

PEC performance of BiVO4, CQDs/BiVO4 and OV-CQDs/BiVO4 measured in 0.5 M KPi under illumination of 100 mW cm−2. (a) The chopped J-V curves for water oxidation, (b) comparison of the chopped J-V curves over the potential range of 0.2–1.2 VRHE obtained from (a), (c) ABPE and (d) IPCE.

(a) Bulk charge separation efficiencies (η bulk), (b) surface charge transfer efficiencies (η surface), (c) Mott-Schottky plots under dark and (d) EIS Nyquist plots under illumination of 100 mWcm−2 at 0.8 VRHE of BiVO4, CQDs/BiVO4 and OV-CQDs/BiVO4.
(a) J-V curves of BiVO4, CQDs/BiVO4 and OV-CQDs/BiVO4 for water oxidation under dark, (b) Tafel plots derived from (a).

(a) IMPS Nyquist plots, (b) electron transfer time (τ c), (c) transient photocurrent decay curves under illumination of 100 mWcm−2 at 1.23 VRHE and (d) electron lifetime (τ r) of BiVO4, CQDs/BiVO4 and OV-CQDs/BiVO4.
Schematic mechanism of the PEC water oxidation performance enhancement of the OV-CQDs/BiVO4 photoanode.
Summary of interfacial charge transfer resistance (R ct), flat band potential (V F), charge carrier density (N D), electron transfer time (τ c) and lifetime (τ r) of the photoanodes.

head.ce:title: Oxygen vacancy-abundant carbon quantum dots as superfast hole transport channel for vastly improving surface charge transfer efficiency of BiVO4 photoanode

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Environmental Science and Engineering, Key Laboratory of Thin Film and Microfabrication Technology (Ministry of Education), Shanghai Jiao Tong University, No. 800 Dongchuan Rd., Shanghai 200240, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Environmental Science and Engineering
Key Laboratory of Thin Film and Microfabrication Technology (Ministry of Education)

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Environmental Science and Engineering, Key Laboratory of Thin Film and Microfabrication Technology (Ministry of Education), Shanghai Jiao Tong University, No. 800 Dongchuan Rd., Shanghai 200240, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The efficiency of hole extraction and transfer at electrode/electrolyte interface is one of the most important bottlenecks of BiVO4 photoanodes for photoelectrochemical (PEC) water splitting. Here, a huge improvement of surface charge transfer efficiency (η surface) of BiVO4 photoanode was achieved by surface modification of oxygen vacancy-abundant carbon quantum dots (OV-CQDs), in which the OV-CQDs serve as superfast hole transport channel for the fact that the abundant OV in OV-CQDs could induce the outward driving force for hole trapping and migration at electrode/electrolyte interface. The OV-CQDs/BiVO4 shows the η surface value of 74.3% at 0.65 V vs. RHE (VRHE), which is 7.1 and 3.3 times higher than BiVO4 and CQDs/BiVO4, respectively. Besides, the OV-CQDs efficiently promote the bulk charge separation and UV-vis light harvesting of BiVO4. Therefore, the OV-CQDs/BiVO4 exhibits remarkable photocurrent densities of 2.76 mA cm−2 at 0.65 VRHE and 4.01 mA cm−2 at 1.23 VRHE, which are 12.5 and 3.4 times higher than BiVO4, 3.5 and 2.6 times higher than CQDs/BiVO4, respectively.

ce:section.ce:para: Hydrogen generation by photoelectrochemical (PEC) water splitting can provide clean and sustainable fuel supply, which is regarded as one of the most promising technologies to mitigate energy crisisandenvironmental emission issues [1–5]. The photoanode materials determine the rate-limiting step of a PEC device, because the oxygen evolution reaction (OER) is far more kinetically sluggish than the hydrogen evolution reaction (HER) [6–8]. Among the metal oxide semiconductors, monoclinic bismuth vanadate (BiVO4) is one of the most promising photoanode candidates due to its relatively small band gap (E g, 2.4–2.5 eV) that enables 11% light harvesting of the solar spectrum, low cost, proper band-edge positions and good stability [7,9,10]. However, the bare BiVO4 photoanode suffers from severe surface charge recombination and sluggish interfacial OER kinetics, seriously inhibiting the PEC performance [1,9,11–13].

Load of oxygen evolution catalysts (OECs) is the most efficient approach for promoting the surface hole transport and improving the OER kinetics at electrode/electrolyte interface. The widely studied and applied OECs for photoanodes are transition-metal-based catalysts, such as transition metaloxides (Co3O4 [9], CoOx [5,7], NiO [5], FeOx [14], FeCoOx [7], NiFeOx [10], etc.), transition metal hydroxides (FeOOH [6,15], NiOOH [15,16], CoOOH [17], Co(OH)x [11], etc.), transition layered double hydroxides (CoNi-LDH [18], CoMn-LDH [19], FeNi-LDH [20], CoAl-LDH [21], ZnCoV-LDH [12], etc.), transition metal phosphates (Fe-Pi [4], Co-Pi [22,23], etc.), transition metal compounds (CoBi [24], etc.), etc. However, high-active metal-free OECs have been rarely explored and reported.

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) as a unique metal-free optoelectronic material have inspired extensive attention for the advantages of eco-friendiness, nontoxicity, low cost, simple synthesis, chemicalinertness [25–27]. CQDs have been applied as avarietyof functional components in photocatalysts, such as photosensitizers [16,28,29], electron acceptors and electron storage mediators [25,29–31]. There is very few report on CQDs acting as hole acceptors, because the driving force in the traditional CQDs for hole extraction and transfer at electrode/electrolyte interface is very weak [16,20]. It has been reported that creating oxygen vacancies (OV) in OECs is an efficient strategy to improve the OER activity, becausethe OV could induce the outward driving force for hole trapping and migration at the electrode/electrolyte interface [32]. For examples, the OV-rich β-FeOOH/BiVO4 photoanode reported by B. Zhang et al. [32] exhibited enhanced PEC water oxidation photocurrent density of 4.25 mA cm−2 at 1.23 V vs. RHE (VRHE) in comparison to the amorphous-FeOOH/BiVO4 photoanode (2.25 mA cm−2). Inspired of the above discussion, the CQDs containing abundant OV may serve as efficient hole acceptors for significantly promoting the interfacial hole extraction and transfer.

Herein, the OV-abundant CQDs (OV-CQDs) were prepared by one-step alkali-assisted ultrasonication. For comparison, the CQDs with little OV (CQDs) were also prepared by hydrothermal method. With surface modification of the above CQDs on BiVO4, the OV-CQDs/BiVO4 and CQDs/BiVO4 were prepared. The OV-CQDs/BiVO4 shows the surface charge transfer efficiency (η surface) of 74.3% at 0.65 VRHE, which is 7.1 and 3.3 times higher than BiVO4 and CQDs/BiVO4, respectively. Systematic studies reveal that the dramatically improved η surface should be ascribed to the fact that the OV-CQDs serve as superfast hole transport channel on BiVO4 surface, in which the abundant OV in OV-CQDs could induce the outward driving force for hole trapping and migration at the electrode/electrolyte interface. Besides, the OV-CQDs efficiently promote the bulk charge separation and UV-vis light harvesting of BiVO4. Therefore, the OV-CQDs/BiVO4 exhibits remarkable photocurrent densities of 2.76 mA cm−2 at 0.65 VRHE and 4.01 mA cm−2 at 1.23 VRHE, which are 12.5 and 3.4 times higher than BiVO4, 3.5 and 2.6 times higher than CQDs/BiVO4, respectively.

All reagents were analytical grade and were used directly without further purification. First, the fluorine-doped tin oxide (FTO) coated glasses (1 cm × 5 cm, 7 Ω/cm2) were ultrasonically washed by acetone, ethanol and deionized water for 30 min, respectively. The BiVO4 photoanodes were prepared according to a modified method as originally developed by Kim and Choi [15]. Briefly speaking, 0.04 M bismuth nitrate pentahydrate (Bi(NO3)3·5H2O, 99%) was dissolved into 50 mL of 0.4 M potassium iodide (KI) with pH of 1.7 adjusted by adding nitric acid (HNO3). Afterwards, 20 mL of ethanol containing 0.23 M p-benzoquinone was added to the above solution with vigorously stirring for 1 min. The electrodeposition of bismuth oxyiodide (BiOI) electrodes were carried out potentiostatically at −0.1 V vs. Ag/AgCl for 5 min at room temperature, using FTO substrates as the working electrodes. The BiVO4 photoanodes were prepared by dropping 400 μL of dimethylsulfoxide (DMSO) solution containing 0.2 M vanadium acetylacetone oxygen (VO(acac)2) onto the surface of BiOI electrodes and then heating to 450 °C for 2 h in a muffle furnace (ramping rate = 2 °C/min), followed by soaking them in 1 M sodium hydroxide (NaOH) solution for 0.5 h. Finally, the pure BiVO4 photoanodes were rinsed with deionized water and dried in air.

The OV-CQDs were prepared by one-step alkali-assisted ultrasonication [33]. Typically, 50 mL of 1 M NaOH solution and 50 mL of 1 M glucose were mixed, followed by ultrasonication for 5 hat room temperature. The obtained solution was adjusted to pH = 7 by dropping hydrochloric acid (HCl) and was diluted by deionized water. For comparison, the CQDs with little OV (CQDs) were prepared by hydrothermal method [28]. 2 g of sucrose was dissolved in 30 mL of deionized water with continuous stirring for 30 min. The above solution was subsequently poured into a 40 mL Teflon lined stainless autoclave and heated to 180 ℃ for 5 h. After cooling to room temperature, the CQDs were obtained by collecting the clear liquid after centrifugationat 10000 rpm for 10 min. The CQDs and OV-CQDs powders were prepared by putting the CQDs and OV-CQDs solution in electric oven at 100 ℃ for 4 days and then milling.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of OV-CQDs/BiVO4 and CQDs/BiVO4 photoanodes
ce:section.ce:para: The OV-CQDs/BiVO4 and CQDs/BiVO4 photoanodes were prepared by dropping 0.5 mL of the above OV-CQDs and CQDs solution on the surface of BiVO4 photoanodes, respectively, followed by spin-coating at 1000 rpm for 10 s, and then dried in air at 100 °C for 30 min on a hot plate. The procedure was repeated for two times.

The morphology and composition were examined by field emission scanning electron microscope (FE-SEM, Ultra Plus, Zeiss, Germany) and high-resolution transmission electronic microscopy (HRTEM; Tecnai G2 F30 S-TWIN, FEI, USA) with energy dispersive X-ray spectrometer (EDX). The crystalline structures were characterized by X-ray diffraction (XRD; AXS-8 Advance, Bruker, Germany) with a Cu Kα (λ = 0.15406 nm) source. The photoluminescence (PL) emission spectra were obtained on a FLS-920 (Edinburgh Instruments). Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra were recorded with a Nicolet iS5 FTIR Spectrometer (Thermo Fisher Scientific) scanning from 4000 to 400 cm−1 with a resolution of 4 cm−1. The light absorption spectra were recorded with a UV-vis spectrophotometer (TU1900, Beijing Purkinje General Instrument Co, China). The surface elemental compositions and states were analyzed by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, AXIS Ultra DLD, Kratos, Japan) with an Al Kα source (hν = 1486.6 eV). The C ls of polluting carbon is used as the benchmark peak for surface charge calibration. The EPR spectra were measured by a Bruker Electron Paramagnetic Resonance Spectrometer (EPR 300E).

The PEC measurements were carried out on an electrochemical workstation (CHI 660D, CH Instruments Inc., USA) using a three-electrode system with a photoanode, a platinum foil and a saturated Ag/AgCl electrode as the working electrode, counter electrode and the reference electrode, respectively, in 0.5 M KPi electrolyte (pH = 7) under solar illumination intensity of 100 mW cm−2 provided by a 350-W xenon lamp with AM 1.5G (Shanghai Hualun Bulb Factory). The KPi electrolyte was prepared by adding KOH to 0.5 M potassium dihydrogen phosphate (KH2PO4) until the pH is 7. All the experiments were performed at ambient temperature. The measured potentials versus Ag/AgCl electrode were converted to the reversible hydrogen electrode (RHE) scale according to the formula (1):
(1)
E
RHE
=
E
Ag / A g C l
+
0.0591
×
p H + 0.1976
where E RHE represents the converted potential vs. RHE. E Ag/AgCl is the obtained potential vs. Ag/AgCl. The scan rate of the photocurrent-potential (J-V) curves was 50 mV/s. The electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was performed with amplitude perturbations of 5 mV and a frequency ranging from 1 Hz to 10 kHz under AM 1.5G simulated solar light (100 mW cm−2). The intensity-modulated photocurrent spectroscopies (IMPS) were performed using an electrochemical workstation (Zennium; effect-Elektrik, Germany) equiped with an illumination intensity-modulated photospectroscopy (CIMPS, PP211) and a white-light lamp (WLC02; Zahner-Elektrik) that allowed the superimposition of sinusoidal modulation (∼10 %) on DC illumination. The modulation frequency of 10 mHz-1 kHz, the illumination intensity of 40 mW cm−2 and the potential of 1.23 V vs. RHE were set.

The TEM images of the CQDs and OV-CQDs indicate the similar size distribution with the average size of ∼1.0 nm, in which the HRTEM images show the lattice spacings of 0.23 nm for OV-CQDs and 0.32 nm for CQDs, corresponding to the (100) and (002) facets of graphite (Fig. 1
a and Fig. S1) [28,34]. The PL emission spectra were performed to evaluate the surface defects of CQDs and OV-CQDs (Fig. S2). The emission wavelength of CQDs and OV-CQDs both shifts to higher value as the excitation wavelength increases, which is characteristic of surface defect emission derived from CQDs and OV-CQDs [28,34]. However, two obvious differences can be observed for the PL spectra of CQDs and OV-CQDs. i) The excitation wavelength that results in the highest intensity of the emission peak wavelength is 400 nm for OV-CQDs, which is smaller than that for CQDs (450 nm). ii) The excitation wavelength of 350–600 nm results in the emission peak wavelength of 444–629 nm for OV-CQDs, which is wider than that for CQDs (468–575 nm). The results imply that more defects exist on the surface of OV-CQDs than that of CQDs.

The EPR technique was further used to study the defects (Fig. 1 b). The EPR signal can be hardly observed for CQDs, while the intense EPR signal with g = 2.002 can be clearly observed for OV-CQDs, suggesting the existence of abundant defects in OV-CQDs. It has been reported that the g = 2.002 for EPR signal is usually ascribed to electrons trapped in OV [35–38]. According to the XPS full spectra shown in Fig. S3, strong C and O signals can be observed for CQDs and OV-CQDs with the O content of 30.2 at% and 22.8 at%, respectively, indicate that abundant oxygen-related species exist on the surface. Noticeably, the surface O content of OV-CQDs is much lower than that of CQDs. Therefore, it is reasonable to deduce that the observed defects are likely to be OV.

The FTIR spectra were used to confirm the oxygen-containing functional groups in CQDs and OV-CQDs. As shown in Fig. 1 c, the characteristic absorption bands of O–H at 3406 cm−1, the stretching vibration bands of C–O at 1074 cm−1, and the stretching vibration bands of CO at 1668 cm−1 for CQDs and 1594 cm−1 for OV-CQDs indicate the presence of carboxylic acid and other oxygen-containing functional groups in CQDs and OV-CQDs [39]. Additionally, the obvious absorption peaks at 2937 and 1414 cm−1 are associated with the stretching vibration CH and CC, respectively [39]. Compared with CQDs, the obviously decreased signal of C–O for OV-CQDs implies that the Ov might be originated from the CO group.

The high-resolution O 1 s XPS spectra of pure CQDs and OV-CQDs powder are shown in Fig. S4. For the O 1 s spectrum of pure CQDs powder, only one peak centered at the binding energy (BE) of 532.6 eV is observed, which is attributed to OC [10,32]. For the O 1 s spectra of the pure OV-CQDs powder, two peaks can be clearly identified, in which the BE at 532.5 eV is attributed to the OC and the lower BE at 531.2 eV is assigned to the OV [10,32]. According to the peak area, the relative OV content in OV-CQDs powder is 40.2%, providing evidence that abundant OV exist in OV-CQDs. The XPS results agree well with the PL and EPR data.

The crystal structures of the samples were first characterized by XRD (Fig. S5). Apart from the signals of FTO (PDF#46-1088), all peaks are well are indexed to monoclinic BiVO4 with no impurity peaks (PDF#14-0688). No any typical carbon signal was observed for CQDs/BiVO4 and OV-CQDs/BiVO4, which is possibly attributed to the relatively low content. The SEM images of BiVO4, CQDs/BiVO4 and OV-CQDs/BiVO4 are compared in Fig. 1 d and Fig. S6, respectively, in which the nanorods surface of CQDs/BiVO4 and OV-CQDs/BiVO4 is a little rough than that of the pure BiVO4. The CQDs or OV-CQDs decoration has an ignorable effect on the thickness of BiVO4 (1.22 μm, Fig. 1 e and Fig. S7). The chemical composition and distribution of BiVO4, CQDs/BiVO4 and OV-CQDs/BiVO4 were examined by EDX spectra and elemental mappings (Figs. S8-S13). Without detecting any carbon signal for the bare BiVO4, the obvious carbon signals were detected for CQDs/BiVO4 (11.89 wt%) and OV-CQDs/BiVO4 (12.62 wt%), suggesting the successful decoration of CQDs or OV-CQDs on BiVO4.

The TEM image of OV-CQDs/BiVO4 shows that the OV-CQDs are uniformly distributed on BiVO4 (Fig. 1 f). The typical lattice fringe spacings of 0.31 nm and 0.18 nm can be observed from the HRTEM of OV-CQDs/BiVO4, corresponding to the (112) and (220) crystal planes of the monoclinic BiVO4 (Fig. 1 g) [40–42]. Meanwhile, the lattice spacings of 0.23 nm and 0.32 nm can be also observed, corresponding to the (100) and (002) facets ofgraphite, respectively [6,34]. The elemental mapping of OV-CQDs/BiVO4 shown in Fig. 1 h gives four elements of O, V, Bi and C, in which the C element is evenly distributed with the weight ratio of 7.86 wt% (Fig. S14), showing that the OV-CQDs were uniformly decorated on the BiVO4.

As shown in Fig. S15, the peaks located at 733 cm−1 and 836 cm−1 can be observed for all samples, which are originated from the tensile vibration peaks of V-O in BiVO4 [21]. Two distinctive peaks at 1640 cm−1 and 3465 cm−1 appear after modification of CQDs or OV-CQDs, which belong to the bending vibration of H2O and the stretching vibration of OH, respectively [21]. Additionally, new peak centered at 1080 cm−1 that is attributed to the stretching vibration bands of CO appears after decorating CQDs or OV-CQDs on BiVO4 [39]. The FTIR results clearly indicate that the CQDs or OV-CQDs are successfully loaded on BiVO4.

The effect of CQDs and OV-CQDs on the structure of BiVO4 was characterized by XPS. Compared with BiVO4, the Bi 4f and V 2p peaks of CQDs/BiVO4 are shifted to higher BE, which suggests the strong interaction between the BiVO4 and CQDs because of the formation of Bi–C and V–C bonds (Fig. 2
a–b) [34]. Compared with BiVO4, the Bi 4f, V 2p and O 1 s peaks of OV-CQDs/BiVO4 all display slight shifts toward lower BE, which is ascribed to the abundant OV in OV-CQDs that induce an electron transfer from OV-CQDs to BiVO4 (Fig. 2 a–c) [43]. The strong interaction between BiVO4 and OV-CQDs due to the formation of Bi–C and V–C bonds could lead to higher BE compared with the pure BiVO4. Meanwhile, the abundant OV in OV-CQDs give rise to lower BE of OV-CQDs/BiVO4 compared with that of the pure BiVO4. Compared with the pure BiVO4, negative shift of BE is observed for OV-CQDs/BiVO4, because the OV in OV-CQDs have dominant role influencing the shift of BE. The formation of Bi–C and V–C bonds is beneficial to the interfacial charge transfer and thus PEC enhancement for CQDs/BViO4 and OV-CQDs/BViO4 [36]. The O 1 s peaks of CQDs/BiVO4 and OV-CQDs/BiVO4 can be fitted to three peaks located at 529.2–529.5, 531.1–531.2, and 532.0–532.3 eV, corresponding to O2− species in the lattice (OL), oxygen vacancy regions (OV) and chemisorbed or dissociated oxygen species from water molecules (OC) [10,32]. Based on the peak areas, the ratios of OV in BiVO4, CQDs/BiVO4 and OV-CQDs/BiVO4 are calculated to be 6.4%, 7.0% and 38.9%, respectively (Table S1).

As shown in Fig. 2 d, the C1s spectra of CQDs/BiVO4 and OV-CQDs/BiVO4 can be deconvoluted into three peaks, which are corresponding to the CC (284.7 eV), CO (285.9 eV), and OCO groups (288.0 eV), respectively [6,34]. A weak C1s spectrum of BiVO4 can only be deconvoluted into one peak (CC), which is ascribed to the surface contamination. The results suggest the succesful modification of CQDs or OV-CQDs on BiVO4. Additionally, the ratios of CC, CO and OCO groups in CQDs/BiVO4 and OV-CQDs/BiVO4 are listed in Table S2, in which the content of CO and OCO groups in OV-CQDs/BiVO4 are obviously lower than that in CQDs/BiVO4, which is consistent with the FTIR results in Fig. 1 c, again implying that the OV in OV-CQDs/BiVO4 possibly originate from the surface C-O group.

The UV-vis absorption spectra were measured to probe the effect of CQDs and OV-CQDs on the optical property of BiVO4. Compared with the bare BiVO4, the CQDs/BiVO4 and OV-CQDs/BiVO4 both show similar and significantly improved visble light harvesting (Fig. 3 ). Compared with BiVO4 and CQDs/BiVO4, the OV-CQDs/BiVO4 show greatly improved ultraviolet light harvesting. After coupling with CQDs or OV-CQDs on BiVO4, the absorptin edge of BiVO4 is shifted from 502 nm to 521 nm. The E g of a semiconductor can be calculated by the Eq. (2) [44]:
(2)
α h v = A
hv -
E g
n / 2
Where α, h, v, and A are the absorption coefficient, Planck's constant, light frequency and proportionality constant, respectively. The n value is determined by the features of the transition for a semiconductor: n = 1 for direct transition and n = 4 for indirect transition. BiVO4 is a direct band gap semiconductor with the n value of 1 [45]. Therefore, the E g value of of BiVO4 is calculated to be 2.47 eV (inset in Fig. 3).

The J-V curves of the photoanodes for PEC water oxidation were measured in 0.5 M KPi electrolyte. As shown in Fig. 4
a, the bare BiVO4 only exhibits the photocurrent densities of 0.22 and 1.13 mA cm−2 at 0.65 and 1.23 VRHE, respectively. After decorating CQDs on BiVO4, the photocurrent densities are enhanced to 0.82 and 1.53 mA cm−2 at 0.65 and 1.23VRHE, respectively. Encouragingly, after modifying OV-CQDs on BiVO4, the photocurrent densities are increased to 2.76 mA cm−2 at 0.65 VRHE and 4.01 mA cm−2 at 1.23 VRHE, which are 12.5 and 3.4 times higher than BiVO4, 3.5 and 2.6 times higher than CQDs/BiVO4, respectively.

The phenomenon of the charging and discharging of surface states for the photoanodes can be reflected by the transient cathodic peaks due to the trapped holes by surface states [44]. As shown in Fig. 4 b, the pristine BiVO4 shows obviously transient cathodic peaks below 0.60 VRHE, indicating the existence of surface states on BiVO4 that result in charging and discharging. However, the transient cathodic peaks can be ignored compared with the transient anodic peaks, indicating that the surface holes mainly undergo recombination. After modification of CQDs or OV-CQDs on BiVO4, the transient cathodic peaks almost disappear, suggesting that surface modification of CQDs or OV-CQDs on BiVO4 can effectively passivates the surface states of BiVO4 and that the surface capacitor charging can be negligible. Therefore, it is reasonable to use transient anodic spike to extract the information on hole recombination behavior at the electrode surface. Under sudden illumination towards the electrode surface, the transient anodic spikes appear for BiVO4 and CQDs/BiVO4, because the holes reach the electrode surface and immediately recombine with the electrons [6,34,44]. No transient spike can be observed for OV-CQDs, suggesting the OV-CQDs efficiently promote the hole transfer at the electrode surface.

The photoelectric conversion capacities of the photoanodes were evaluated by the applied bias photon-to-current efficiency (ABPE) and the incident photon-to-current conversion efficiency (IPCE) measurements. Supposing 100% of Faradaic efficiency, the ABPE can be calculated by the Eq. (3) [44]:
(3)
A B P E
%
=
J
1.23 - V
P
× 100
where J is the photocurrent density (mA cm−2), V is the applied bias (VRHE) and P is the incident illumination power density (100 mW cm−2). As shown in Fig. 4 c, the maximum ABPE of OV-CQDs/BiVO4 reaches up to 1.60% at 0.58 VRHE, which is much higher than that of BiVO4 (0.16% at 0.85 VRHE) and CQDs/BiVO4 (0.50% at 0.48 VRHE). The IPCE was measured at 1.23 VRHE and was calculated by the Eq. (4) [44]:
(4)
I P C E
%
=
1240 J
λ
λ P
λ
× 100
where J(λ), λ and P(λ) are the measured photocurrent density at specific wavelength, the wavelength of incident light and the measured light power density at that wavelength, respectively. As shown in Fig. 4 d, the maximum IPCE of OV-CQDs/BiVO4 reaches up to 73.0% at the wavelength of 300 nm, which is much higher than that of BiVO4 (12.0% at 340 nm) and CQDs/BiVO4 (24.9% at 330 nm). The pristine BiVO4 shows little photocurrent response at the wavelength above 500 nm. With modification of CQDs or OV-CQDs on BiVO4, the photocurrent response is efficiently increased.

To reveal the reasons of greatly increased photocurrent of OV-CQDs/BiVO4, the bulk charge separation efficiencies (η bulk) and surface charge transfer efficiencies (η surface) of the photoanodes are calculated by the Eqs. (5) and (6), respectively [6,34,44]:
(5)
η
bulk
=
J
N
a 2
S
O 3
J
abs
(6)
η
surface
=
J
H 2
O
J
N
a 2
S
O 3
where JH2O (Fig. 4 a) and J Na2SO3 (Fig. S16) are the photocurrent densities achieved by the oxidation of H2O and Na2SO3, respectively. J abs is the photocurrent density calculated by assuming that the absorbed photons are completely converted to current density, which can be estimated by combining AM 1.5G solar spectrum (Fig. S17 a) and light harvesting efficiency (LHE, Fig. S17 b) according to the Eqs. (7) and (8) [6,34]:
(7)
J
abs
=
∫
300
λ e
λ 1240
N
ph
λ
L H E
λ
d λ
(8)
LHE = 1 -
10
- A
λ
where λ is the wavelength (nm); A(λ) is the absorbance at wavelength λ; λ e is the absorption edge wavelength of the photoanodes; N ph(λ) is the photon flux (mW cm−2 nm−1). The J abs values of BiVO4, CQDs/BiVO4 and OV-CQDs/BiVO4 are calculated to be 5.60, 6.77 and 6.94 mA cm−2, respectively (Fig. S18). As shown in Fig. 5
a, the η bulk values of CQDs/BiVO4 and OV-CQDs/BiVO4 are improved in comparison to that of BiVO4 below 1.10 VRHE. As shown in Fig. 5 b, the OV-CQDs/BiVO4 shows the η surface values of 74.3% at 0.65 VRHE and 73.4% at 1.23 VRHE, which are 7.1 and 2.9 times higher than BiVO4, and 3.3 and 2.7 times higher than CQDs/BiVO4, respectively. The results indicates that the OV-CQDs can significantly accelerate the surface OER kinetics.

To investigate the electronic properties, the Mott-Schottky plots of the samples were measured. The positive slope of BiVO4 indicates the character of n-type semiconductor (Fig. 5 c). The flat band potential (V F) and charge carrier density (N D) can be obtained according to the Eqs. (9) and (10), respectively [44]:
(9)
1
C 2
=
2
e ε
ε 0
N D
V -
V
FB
-
K B
T
e
(10)
N D
=
2
e ε
ε 0
d
1
C 2
dV
- 1
where C is the space charge capacitance; e is the fundamental electron charge (1.6×10−19 C); ε is the relative permittivity of the semiconductor (68 for BiVO4 [10]); ε0 is the permittivity of vacuum (8.86×10−12 F/m); V is the applied potential. The V F value of BiVO4 is 0.231 VRHE, while the V F values of CQDs/BiVO4 and OV-CQDs/BiVO4 are 0.093 VRHE. The negative shift of V F implies increased band bending, which induces larger driving force for interfacial charge transfer [14]. The N D value of OV-CQDs/BiVO4 is 21.0×1020 cm−3, which is 2.49 and 1.24 times higher than that of BiVO4 (8.43×1020 cm−3) and CQDs/BiVO4 (16.9×1020 cm−3), respectively. The higher N D value indicates enhanced electronic conductivity and better charge separation property [10].

To understand the interfacial charge transfer kinetics, the EIS Nyquist plots of the photoanodes were measured under illumination. As shown in Fig. 5 d, only one semicircle is observed for each sample, suggesting the only one time constant [10]. Therefore, the EIS data can be fitted by an equivalent circuit model as shown in the inset of Fig. 5 d, where the R s and R ct represent the series resistance and interfacial charge transfer resistance, respectively [10,24]. The fitted results of the photoanodes are listed in Table S3. The OV-CQDs/BiVO4 has the smallest R ct value of 133.2 Ω, compared with BiVO4 (580.9 Ω) and CQDs/BiVO4 (339.4 Ω), indicating the easiest charge transfer at the OV-CQDs/BiVO4/electrolyte interface.

To further confirm the OER activities of the photoanodes, the electrocatalytic J-V curves for water oxidation were measured. For ease of comparison, the OER activity of a catalyst can be assessed by the overpotential at the current density of 10 mA cm−2, which is defined as the potential (vs. RHE) at 10 mA cm−2 minus 1.23 [46]. As shown in Fig. 6
a, the overpotential of OV-CQDs/BiVO4 at the current density of 10 mA cm−2 is 1.21 VRHE, which is much lower than that of BiVO4 (1.51 VRHE) and CQDs/BiVO4 (1.44 VRHE). The Tafel slope is another important indicator to evaluate the OER activity of a catalyst. As shown in Fig. 6 b, the Tafel slope of OV-CQDs/BiVO4 is 153 mV dec−1, which is much smaller than that of BiVO4 (491 mV dec−1) and CQDs/BiVO4 (270 mV dec−1). The results again indicate the OV-CQDs modification can greatly promote the interfacial OER kinetics of BiVO4 electrode.

To gain deep insights into the effect of OV-CQDs and CQDs on the charge transfer and recombination behaviors in BiVO4, the electron transfer time (τ c) and lifetime (τ r) of the photoanodes were further evaluated. The τ c obtained from the IMPS Nyquist plot represents the average time required for electrons transport from the photoanode to the FTO substrate, which is calculated by the Eq. (11) [34,44]:
(11)
τ c
=
1
2 π
f min
where f min is the frequency at the imaginary minimum in the IMPS Nyquist plots (Fig. 7
a). As shown in Fig. 7 b, the OV-CQDs/BiVO4 shows a shorter τ c value (1.83 ms) than BiVO4 (4.32 ms) and CQDs/BiVO4 (3.39 ms), indicating the best electron transport property in OV-CQDs/BiVO4.

As shown in Fig. 7 c, the transient photocurrent decay curves were measured to study the charge recombination behaviors in the photoanodes, which were quantificationally reflected by using the parameter D as follows [9,47,48]:
(12)
D
=
I t
-
I s
/
I m
-
I s
where I t, I s and I m are the time-dependent photocurrent, the steady-state photocurrent and the transientphotocurrentspike, respectively. The τ r is defined as the time at which lnD = −1. As shown in Fig. 7 d, the τ r value of OV-CQDs/BiVO4 is 2.66 s, which is 9.5 and 6.5 times higher than that of BiVO4 (0.28 s) and CQDs/BiVO4 (0.41 s), respectively, suggesting a lower charge recombination rate in OV-CQDs/BiVO4. The R ct, V F, N D, τ c and τ r of the photoanodes are summarized in Table 1 .

For n-type semiconductor, the conduction band (CB) locates at the position that is 0.1–0.3 eV higher than the VFB position [34,44]. Assuming potential difference between the CB andVFB is 0.15 eV, the CB potential of BiVO4 is 0.081 eVRHE according to the VFB value of BiVO4 (Fig. 5 c). Therefore, the valence band (VB) potential of BiVO4 is 2.551 eVRHE. The absorption edge of OV-CQDs locates at approximately 636.9 nm, giving the E g of 1.95 eV (Fig. S19 a). The Lowest Unoccupied Molecular Orbital (LUMO) level of OV-CQDs was determined by cyclic voltammetry (CV), which was measured using the bare FTO and OV-CQDs solution as the work electrode and electrolyte, respectively [6,34,49,50]. As shown in Fig. S19 b, the LUMO level of OV-CQDs is estimated to be −0.33 VRHE, indicating that the Highest Occupied Molecular Orbital (HOMO) level of OV-CQDs is 1.62 VRHE. Therefore, the type-II energy band alignment is confirmed for OV-CQDs/BiVO4 heterojunction (Fig. S20).

On the basis of above results, a possible mechanism for explaining the PEC water oxidation performance enhancement of the OV-CQDs/BiVO4 photoanode is illustrated in Fig. 8 . The OV-CQDs serve as visible light sensitizers that improve the visible light harvesting of BiVO4 photoanode due to the up-conversion effect [27,34,51]. When the OV-CQDs/BiVO4 photoanode is illuminated by AM 1.5G soalr light, the photogenerated electrons and holes are generated by BiVO4 and OV-CQDs, in which the photoinduced holes in the VB of BiVO4 are injected into the HOMO level of OV-CQDs for oxygen evolution, while the photoinduced electrons in the LUMO level of OV-CQDs transfer to the CB of BiVO4 and further reach the Pt electrode through an external circuit for hydrogen production. However, the main limiting factor for the PEC performance enhancement of BiVO4 and CQDs/BiVO4 is the severe surface charge recombination due to the sluggish interfacial OER kinetics. When the OV-CQDs are modified on the BiVO4 surface, the holes quickly transfer to the photoanode surface and are efficiently injected into electrolyte for water oxidation, because the abundant OV in OV-CQDs could induce the outward driving force for hole trapping and migration at the electrode/electrolyte interface. Therefore, in the OV-CQDs/BiVO4 system, the OV-CQDs not only promote bulk charge separation and UV-vis light harvesting, but also serve as ultrafast hole transport channel that greatly accelerates the OER kinetics for water oxidation.

In summary, we prepared the OV-CQDs/BiVO4 by modifying OV-CQDs on BiVO4 photoanode, which exhibits significantly enhanced PEC performance in comparison to BiVO4 and CQDs/BiVO4. This is mainly because the OV-CQDs serve as superfast hole transport channel for dramatically improving the η surface of BiVO4, in which the OV in OV-CQDs could induce the outward driving force for hole trapping and migration at the electrode/electrolyte interface. Additionally, the OV-CQDs efficiently promote the bulk charge separation and UV-vis light harvesting. This new findings shed light on the role and mechanism of the OV in OV-CQDs for greatly improving the OER activity, which will pave the way toward the important and potential applications of OV-CQDs as OECs for PEC or electrochemical water splitting.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors would like to acknowledge the National Key Research and Development Program of China (Nos. 2018YFE0122300 and 2018YFB1502001), the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 22176125 and 22076121), Shanghai International Science and Technology Cooperation Fund Project (No. 18520744900), China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (No. 2021M692064) and SJTU-AEMD for support.
